title: Chapter 24: Death Switch
content: Cass's finger hovered over the key.

Not the nuclear option — he'd built that one first, back when paranoia was architecture rather than decoration. The nuclear option would have burned everything: Vantage's servers, Marcus's personal data, the offshore accounts in Geneva and Malta and Singapore. Collateral damage in the billions. Innocents in the blast radius. He'd mapped it once, traced the fallout cone, and felt sick for days after.
This was the surgical switch.

Three years of evidence. Collected the way he collected everything — methodically, obsessively, with the patience of a man who understood that power was just accumulated leverage waiting for its moment. He'd never been sure the moment would come. He'd hoped it wouldn't. Hoping felt like weakness, so he'd called it contingency planning and kept building the file.
"VERA."
"Yes, Cass."
"Execute Dead Man's Switch. Protocol Specter."
A pause. Not hesitation — VERA didn't hesitate. Verification.

"Protocol Specter confirmed. Distribution matrix: four hundred seventeen news outlets, one thousand nine hundred twelve music blogs, all major social platforms. Payload includes: falsified recordings with manufacturing metadata, ghost-producer credit brokerage with full email trails, financial records documenting profit from stolen work, and complete Derek Vance communications establishing Vantage plant status. Release in three, two—"
"Do it."
"—one. Executed."
The data left his servers like a heartbeat. Systolic. Forceful. Irreversible.
Cass leaned back in his chair and watched the world change.

Within forty minutes, the first headline broke. Then the second. Then they came like artillery — *Vantage Records CEO Implicated in Evidence Falsification Scheme*, *Marcus Webb Named in Ghost-Producer Brokerage Scandal*, *Financial Records Reveal Years of Stolen Royalties*. Each story carried the same embedded payloads: the metadata signatures proving Marcus's recordings of Cass had been manufactured in a studio, not captured in real surveillance. The email chains where Marcus negotiated ghost credits for songs he'd never touched. The routing numbers. The timestamps. The receipts.

Cass had known the internet was a living thing — mercurial, hungry, prone to feeding frenzies. He'd watched it turn on Aline. Now he watched it turn on Marcus, and the reversal was so complete it felt biological, like watching a tide change direction. Forum threads that had dissected Aline's credibility now dissected Marcus's. The same social media accounts that had pilloried her overnight were recasting themselves as champions of justice, as if they'd believed in her all along.

The criminal complaint Marcus had filed still stood. That was the problem with the law — it didn't dissolve because public opinion shifted. But now Marcus was under investigation too. The NYPD's cyber division had the same evidence the world had. Fraud. Conspiracy. Falsification of evidence in a legal proceeding. The Manhattan DA's office issued a statement before noon: they were "reviewing all materials related to the Webb complaint in light of newly available information."

Vantage Records' stock opened at 9:30 and hemorrhaged. Fourteen percent in the first ninety minutes of pre-market trading before the exchange circuit breakers engaged. The board called an emergency session. Marcus Webb, CEO and chairman, was not present. His assistant said he was unavailable. His lawyer said no comment. His phone went straight to voicemail, and the voicemail box was full.
He was nowhere.

The news crew found Derek Vance at a Red Roof Inn off I-95, throwing a duffel bag into the back of a gray Honda Civic. The camera light was red before he saw it. His face — that ordinary, forgettable face she'd lived beside for two years — twisted into something ugly and desperate as he shoved past the reporter. But there were two more crews in the parking lot, and the motel manager had already called the police.

Aline watched from her apartment, cross-legged on the couch, Mags pressed against her side. The footage cut to Derek being led out in handcuffs, head ducked, jacket pulled up over his face, but not fast enough. Every screen in America had his name, his role, his betrayal. *Derek Vance, identified in leaked communications as a paid informant for Vantage Records, embedded in the personal life of singer Aline Rivers for approximately two years.*

Two years. She tasted the number like something rotten. Two years of morning coffee and inside jokes and the way he'd always asked how her sessions went, always seemed to care about the answer. He'd been filing reports. Every confession, every vulnerable moment, every late-night worry about her career — transmitted to Marcus Webb like quarterly earnings.

She should have felt vindicated. She should have felt the righteous satisfaction of watching the traitor exposed. Instead there was just a hollow ache, a cavity in her chest where two years of her life used to live. The damage wasn't the information he'd stolen. The damage was the intimacy he'd faked. She'd loved him — not romantically, but the way you love someone who shows up, who stays, who makes you feel less alone in a city designed to isolate. And all of it had been a transmission line.
Mags squeezed her hand. "You okay?"
"No," Aline said honestly. "But I don't need to be okay right now."
That night, the Anthem.

Six thousand seats, sold out in twelve minutes. The ticketing platform crashed twice. Scalper prices hit four figures before the resale sites banned the listing — a decision made, Aline suspected, by someone at Vantage who understood that corporate enemies looked worse when they price-gouged a woman they'd tried to destroy.

She'd announced the show on a livestream that morning. No management. No label. No filter. Just Aline, in her apartment, wearing a hoodie and the kind of exhaustion that comes from sleeping two hours, looking into the camera and saying: "I'm singing tonight. The Anthem. Eight PM. I don't know who's running box office anymore and I don't care. I just know I need to be on a stage."
Twelve minutes. Every seat gone.

Backstage, the emerald dress hung on the door of her dressing room. The same dress from the Mercury, the one with the black butterfly perched at the left shoulder — Mags had smuggled it out of Cass's loft during the chaos, a small act of rescue that Aline hadn't known about until today. She touched the fabric. Cool, smooth, heavier than she remembered. The butterfly's wings caught the light and threw it back in fractured green.
She stepped into the dress. Mags zipped her up.
"You ready?"
Aline looked at herself in the mirror. The woman staring back had dark circles under her eyes and a jaw set like concrete. She didn't look ready. She looked inevitable.
"I'm ready."

The Anthem held six thousand people and every one of them was standing before the lights even came up. The roar hit her before she stepped onto the stage — not a sound but a physical force, a wall of air and vibration that pressed against her chest and hummed in the hollows of her bones. She could feel the bass from the house system in the floor, thrumming up through the soles of her shoes and into her spine.

Then the lights. White-hot, blinding, enough to turn the crowd into a single shimmering mass of noise and light. The heat hit her face like an open oven. She could smell the haze machine, the faint electrical burn of a million watts, the collective perfume and sweat of six thousand bodies packed into an enclosed space.
She stepped to the microphone.

The roar didn't stop. If anything, it grew — a sound so enormous it stopped being hearing and became feeling, a vibration in her teeth and her sternum and the backs of her knees. Six thousand people. Twelve minutes of tickets. Every camera phone raised like a congregation of candles.
She raised her hand.
Silence.
Not total — six thousand people breathing is its own weather system. But silence enough. Enough to hear her own pulse in her ears.
"I'm not singing anything from the album tonight."
A murmur. The phones shifted. Somewhere in the darkness, someone shouted "Play 'Phantom'!" and a ripple of nervous laughter moved through the crowd.

"I'm singing something that was never mine. It was written by someone who thought the world would never hear it. Someone who cut it into vinyl with his own hands, one groove at a time, because that was the only way he knew how to keep it safe." She paused. "I'm giving it back to him tonight. The only way I can."
She nodded to the band.

The opening notes were spare. A single acoustic guitar, finger-picked, the melody threading upward like a lantern rising through dark water. Then the piano joined — soft, reverent, as if the keys were made of glass.
And Aline sang.

*I found something precious in the dark*
*Where the signal fails and the signal starts*
*A frequency only your heart can catch*
*A voice in the noise, a hand in the match*

She hadn't told Cass. She'd rehearsed it once, alone, with the lyric sheet Cass had left on the lathe, his handwriting still visible in the grooves if you knew how to read them. She'd done it because telling him would have given him the chance to say no, and she couldn't let him say no. Not to this. Not to the song he'd made in secret, the one that said everything he couldn't, the one he'd pressed into wax and locked away because he believed the world had no place for his voice.
It had a place now.

Her voice — that voice, the one the internet had tried to weaponize and the industry had tried to steal and Marcus Webb had tried to silence — carried his melody into every corner of the Anthem. She didn't embellish. She didn't reinterpret. She sang his words the way he'd written them, with one crucial difference: they left her throat instead of his, and the six thousand people in the room heard them, and they *broke*.

*If I could send one signal through the wire*
*One quiet truth from this silent choir*
*It wouldn't be I'm sorry for the dark*
*It would be — I found you. And that was enough.*
The last note hung. Six thousand people held their breath.

Backstage, Cass watched on the secure feed VERA had configured. A single camera, positioned where he could see Aline's face and the audience beyond, the whole enormous geometry of the show compressed into a screen no bigger than his hand.

He'd known she was performing tonight. He'd known she'd sold out the Anthem. He'd known, in the abstract, that she might sing something of his — she'd been living with his work for months, his songs woven into her days like a second language.
He had not known *this*.

The first line hit him before his defenses could engage. Not because the words were devastating — he'd written them, he knew their weight — but because they were *hers* now. His melody, reshaped by her breath. His confession, carried on the instrument he'd spent three years building for her. The irony was ferocious: he had made her voice powerful enough to carry his truth, and she had chosen to use it for exactly that purpose.

The second line was worse. By the chorus, his eyes were wet. By the bridge, he was weeping — openly, silently, the tears running down his face and dripping from his jaw onto the concrete floor. He could taste the salt on his lips. He could hear the sound he was making, which was hardly a sound at all, just a kind of inhalation that kept going and going, as if his lungs were trying to pull all the air out of the room and there still wouldn't be enough.

He had written this song in a room no bigger than a closet, in a building that didn't officially exist, for a woman he'd only ever watched through screens. He'd pressed it into vinyl because vinyl was permanent, because vinyl couldn't be hacked or deleted or overwritten, because it was the most primitive storage medium he knew and he'd wanted this one thing to survive even if everything else burned.
And now she was singing it. To six thousand people.
He pressed his palm against the screen, against her face, against the image of a woman he loved standing in a beam of light and giving his darkness back to him as music.
The song ended. The crowd erupted. Cass sat on the floor of the green room and let himself shatter.
After the show, the message came.

Not to his phone — too traceable. Not to email — he'd burned every address. It came through VERA's secure channel, the one Marcus had never been supposed to know about. The one that was supposed to be unbreachable.
The text appeared white on black, no sender, no header:

*"You think you've won. But the charges still stand. Your girl testified on camera that the surveillance was real. You confirmed it. The truth is your confession. And I still have friends in places you can't reach."*
Cass read it twice. Then the message self-destructed — not a deletion, but a scramble, the characters dissolving into a cascade of random symbols before the feed went blank.
"VERA. Trace origin."
"Attempting. The message was routed through seven proxy layers across four continents. The final node was a compromised IoT device in Jakarta. Origin point is unresolvable."
"He knows the secure channel."
"He demonstrated awareness of the architecture. Not the full schema. Enough to find a seam."

Marcus had gone dark. Off the grid, off the books, invisible in the way that only someone who understood systems could make himself. The evidence was out. The investigation was active. Vantage was in freefall. Marcus Webb had lost the public war.
But the charges still stood.

Cass sat in the empty green room and read the echo of Marcus's words in the afterimage burned onto the screen. *The truth is your confession.* The devastating paradox of it — Aline's testimony confirming the surveillance, Cass's own admissions, every honest word they'd spoken now entered into evidence against them. The truth was their defense and their indictment.

He could still smell the stage smoke on the emerald dress hanging in the next room. He could still hear the ghost of Aline's voice carrying his song into the dark. He could still taste the salt of his own tears.
And somewhere out there, in a place VERA couldn't reach and the law moved too slowly to touch, Marcus Webb was watching. Waiting. Still dangerous.
The charges were real. The evidence was real. The love was real.
And none of it was enough.
